I shared that with our content team.
So they're going to use that to update the company context, the audience personas.
And we also have a products and services artifact in Atlas that we update with the like factual, like the most like recent product information.
But I can actually show you because Kim, I know you weren't, I think you weren't on the call when I showed like Atlas.
To the team, so I can just show you really quickly.
Yeah, sounds good.
Yeah, I don't believe I was.
That'd be helpful.
Yeah, for sure.
Okay, so this is your workspace in Atlas, which is our content generation platform that we built, I think, almost six or seven months ago.
And right now, it's just like a GrowthX tool.
And it includes, like, the artifacts that we use to generate vizcom content.
So we have your company context here, the audience personas, and the writing guidelines.
And this has been reviewed by the vizcom team.
So these are very important during when we're, like, creating content.
The AI directly pulls from these three artifacts.
And we also have a products and services artifact here, which...

The AI uses to just like fact check the draft and make sure that it's not like making any conclusions about vizcom and like drawing like sometimes the AI will just infer that a platform can do something because like it's, you know, like the company context might say that like vizcom helps automate something.
And then like the AI might infer that like this automation applies to like use cases that maybe you don't support.

So these are like the products and services artifact is really there to fact check those claims to ensure that like the product messaging is on point and we're not like just saying things that you don't actually do.
And then, yeah, for each of these artifacts, so we update them pretty regularly using your feedback that comes through like when you're like don't know.
for app.
Reviewing the content, but also like whenever you have a major update to your platform, you can let us know and then we'll update this accordingly.
And we can kind of like go back in history and see like what was the last update.
So the last update was yesterday.
We kind of go back to previous versions and revert as needed.

And then for the article generation pipeline, so we call it agentic because it includes steps where the AI will evaluate its own output and grade it and then improve it if it doesn't meet a specific threshold.

So this is our editors like testing environment, which we do a lot of like experiments internally just to see how different inputs influence the output and like just to like continuously improve like the quality of the articles that you're getting and to allow us to like be.
So we like spin up these like test environments, but this is your article generation pipeline, and it's like consists of multiple steps here.
So as you can see, like we're already working on some topics here and the article generation process really starts with the input, which is the title of the article.

So for example, so in the input we'll give it the title of the article, and then it generates an assignment direction here, which like it will identify who the primary persona is, who the secondary persona is, and the kind of like sections that the article is going to cover.
And then obviously we can go in here and like manually make changes.
As needed, because, like, if we get the outline right, then the output is a lot better as well.

And then once we're happy with the assignment direction, we run the researcher, and the research step uses Tavoli, which is a tool that's similar to perplexity, but we found that it's more powerful and it can find, like, better quality information.
And it's just, like, it's just a, like, we also use EXA, but EXA is applied when we really deal with, like, complex subject matter.
So Tavoli, like, does really great for the majority of our clients, but we can kind of experiment and see if Tavoli isn't working, then we switch to EXA.
So the research step takes the assignment direction, and then it generates questions that it has to, the AI has to answer in order to, like, draft the article.
Bye.

Using this step, it allows us to generate comprehensive in-depth content because if you've ever tried to generate an article in just Claude or ChatGPT and you didn't give it a lot of research or just a comprehensive document, the content that it generates is very shallow.
So you're not going to get a very helpful article by just having the AI give you information.

So what we do here is the AI is going to prepare a list of questions and it's going to answer those questions and then those answers are compiled in like a very comprehensive research document that the AI pulls from as it's writing the article.

And then it's also during this step, the AI, like once it's done researching the questions, then it's going to go back and look at the answers and check to see if those answers actually answered the question.
And if there are any gaps identified, then it's going to go back back.
And try to find additional information to provide an answer to the question that it's trying to answer.
So it's kind of like trying to like improve itself.
Once the research is done, that's when it drafts the article.
And then from here, we add internal links and we also add source links.
The source links is important to like make sure that any stats or like third-party research are appropriately attributed.
And then, but we also like manually fact check this before we send the article to you.
And then for the validate writing guidelines step, this is super important because what we have found is that the AI, like we use Claude.
So Claude will listen to the writing guidelines when it's drafting the article.
However, sometimes the...
Writing guidelines are a little bit long, or we may unintentionally introduce conflicting instructions in the writing guidelines.
So the AI might ignore some parts of those instructions and just not follow them.
And then by adding this extra step here, the AI is going to take the draft and then evaluate it against the writing guidelines.
And it generates a confidence score.
So on a scale of 0 to 1, here, like the confidence score was 0.9.
And for example, like it gives you a summary of the changes.
So it replaced M dashes.
Like it like changes, like it changed like numerical ranges.
So these are like pretty like surface level changes.
But for other articles, there might be issues with like transitions or flow.
So it's going to go back and change the draft.
To fit the writing guidelines and really so that the article really follows the writing guidelines completely.
And then after this step, we run the fact checker, which is actually really, it's like a super important step.
And I'm going to show you how it works on the backend.
So what it does is it will split the article into chunks.
And these chunks represent units of information that it has to verify whether it's true or not.
So, for example, okay, so here's one passage.
So traditional rendering workflows create an impossible choice for product designers.
You sketch 300 concepts, but you can only afford to render 20.
It gives you...
The reason why it selected this passage for fact-checking, and then it generates a question that it has to answer in order to confirm the validity of this passage.
And then it's going to do the same for all of the passages that it has identified as requiring fact-checking, and then it's going to research each of these passages.
So let's see, I mean, yeah, so you kind of have like the questions here, and then you have like the answers, and like the key points that support whether something is true or not.
So, and this is also done using EXA.
So, if anything, like if a piece of information cannot be verified, it's going to remove that piece of information from the draft.
Sometimes, or it will rewrite the sentence.
So, sometimes if...

We're reading an article before delivering it to you, and we see, okay, like a sentence that goes something like, even though many product designers say, like, even though product designers may struggle with some use case, there is no, like, evidence to support this claim, or there is no, like, statistical evidence to support this claim.
And that's a sign for us that the fact checker rewrote a sentence, and we'll just either go in and, like, remove it, or we will, like, do manual research to try to find a data point to replace it.
And then, yeah, after the fact checker is done, then we will read the article and do, like, an in-depth editorial review, and then we send it to you for a review.
So that's kind of the workflow from start to finish.
Cool.
Thank you for running.
No worries.

Yeah, for sure.

think, like, thematically, one of them was just around, like, how we speak about, like, designers obviously using AI.
think there was one line that was, like, it's obvious, like, designers are going to be quick to jump to using, like, AI tools.
I think that language we can maybe reframe as, like, they're curious or, like, they're still exploring, like, what the different applications are.

I think, like, obviously, so, like, with the product session, like, making sure that we're, like, the nomenclature of, like, all our features is right.

So we can create additional custom artifacts.

That's great.
